summary: Gruber argues that Google's decision to drop native H.264 support from Chrome will not drive adoption of WebM, but will instead entrench Flash Player as the default video delivery mechanism. He contrasts an 'idealistic scenario' where video providers dual-encode in H.264 and WebM with a 'practical scenario' where providers simply continue serving H.264 via Flash to Chrome users. He points out that major video providers have already invested heavily in H.264 and have no incentive to take on the additional cost and legal risk of WebM encoding. Apple successfully drove non-Flash video adoption because iOS devices were popular, supported H.264 well, and offered no alternative — Chrome lacks that leverage because Flash is bundled right in.
key_points: Google dropping H.264 from Chrome while keeping Flash bundled means providers will simply serve H.264 via Flash, not switch to WebM
The 'idealistic scenario' requires providers to double storage and encoding costs with no clear business incentive
Apple drove H.264 adoption because iOS offered no Flash fallback, making native H.264 the only option — Chrome has no such leverage
WebM's uncertain patent status makes legal teams at major providers unlikely to embrace it
Marco Arment's 18-month-old prediction that Mozilla's stance would just keep everyone on Flash has proven correct
money_quotes: Let me be clear, though: there is nothing wrong with playing a video in Flash. What there's something wrong with is requiring Flash Player to play video.
If depending upon Flash Player for video playback is fine, then why doesn't Chrome just drop support for HTML5 video entirely?
The least amount of work these companies can do now, to continue serving video to Chrome users, is to keep using H.264-encoded video via Flash Player.
Dropping native H.264 playback from Chrome while still allowing H.264 playback via Flash Player isn't going to drive adoption of WebM. It just means that Chrome users will get H.264 via Flash.
By not supporting the practical format, Mozilla isn't making a brave statement or taking a stand: they're just keeping everyone on Flash and preventing meaningful adoption of HTML5's <video> element.

That third point is key. Some of these companies took on a lot of work in order to serve video to iOS devices, because their existing systems were based entirely on Flash Player. But because Flash Player is not available on iOS, this was still the least possible work they could do.
key_insight: Google's removal of H.264 from Chrome lacks the leverage to force WebM adoption because, unlike Apple's iOS strategy, Chrome still offers a Flash fallback — making it easier for providers to do nothing than to change.
